9.note: Unless the Lord had left us descendants

18.note: Scarlet sins made white as snow — the great multitude who 'washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb'; Isaiah 1:18's cleansing promise is fulfilled through the atoning blood of Christ
The blood of Christ purifies the conscience from dead works; Isaiah 1:18's offer of cleansing from scarlet/crimson sin is grounded in the high-priestly offering of Christ

21.note: The faithful city that became a whore — Revelation applies the harlot-city imagery to Babylon/Rome as the anti-Jerusalem; the contrast with the pure bride of Revelation 21:2 fulfills Isaiah 1:26's promise to restore Zion as the faithful city

25.note: God smelting away dross by fire — Peter applies the refiner's fire to the testing of faith more precious than gold; Isaiah 1:25's purging fire is the eschatological trial that produces proven faith in Christ

The refiner purifying the sons of Levi — Malachi's messenger refining as silver is the same divine purging as Isaiah 1:25, applied to the Levitical priesthood and fulfilled in Christ the great High Priest who purifies his people

1.note: Song of the Vineyard -- Jesus the True Vine replaces unfaithful Israel

Isaiah saw his glory and spoke of him — John identifies the YHWH whose glory Isaiah saw in the throne-vision (Isaiah 6:1-4) with the pre-incarnate Christ; the seraphim's Trisagion is the worship of the Son before the Incarnation

2.note: Six-winged creatures crying Holy holy holy

9.note: You will indeed hear but never understand — Jesus cites Isaiah 6:9-10 to explain his parable-methodology; the hardening effect of his teaching on the religious leaders fulfills the Isaianic commission to preach to those who will not understand

Paul's final quotation of Isaiah 6:9-10 to the Roman Jews who reject the gospel closes the book of Acts — the Isaianic hardening frames the entire mission narrative from Jesus's parable-ministry to Paul's Roman imprisonment

14.note: Behold the virgin shall conceive and bear a son and shall call his name Immanuel — Matthew cites Isaiah 7:14 LXX (parthenos = virgin) as fulfilled in the virginal conception of Jesus; the Immanuel sign given to Ahaz in the Assyrian crisis reaches its ultimate fulfillment in the one who is literally God-with-us

12.note: Do not fear what they fear — sanctify Christ as Lord

14.note: A stone that causes stumbling — to Israel
A stone that causes people to stumble

17.note: I will put my trust in him — I and the children

1.note: Light to Galilee

2.note: The people dwelling in darkness have seen a great light — Matthew applies Isaiah 9:1-2 to Jesus's Galilean ministry; the light in Zebulun and Naphtali is the gospel's arrival in the darkened region; Jesus is the great light

22.note: Though Israel's number be like the sand — a remnant saved

1.note: The root of Jesse who rises to rule the Gentiles — Paul cites Isaiah 11:10 (the root of Jesse as a signal for the nations) as fulfilled in Christ's universal lordship over Gentiles

The Root of David has conquered — Revelation applies the Root of Jesse/David imagery of Isaiah 11:1 to the risen Christ who has conquered; the shoot from the stump of Jesse is the crucified-and-risen Messiah

2.note: The Spirit of the LORD shall rest upon him — the anointing Spirit of Isaiah 11:2 is visibly manifest when the Spirit descends as a dove at Jesus's baptism; the Spirit resting on Christ at baptism marks the beginning of the Isaianic messianic anointing

10.note: The Root of Jesse will stand as a banner for the peoples
The Root of Jesse will stand as a banner for the peoples

8.note: Sudden destruction like labor pains -- day of the Lord

10.note: Sun darkened and stars fall -- day of the Lord imagery

9.note: Babylon has fallen -- the prophetic announcement

6.note: The LORD stationing a watchman to report what he sees anticipates the watching motif of Revelation 3:2-3 (be watchful) and Revelation 16:15 (blessed is the one who stays awake); the watchman's posture is the eschatological stance of the church awaiting Christ's return.

10.note: What the LORD of hosts has declared — the trustworthy word handed to the people crushed and winnowed — parallels Paul's appeal to the certain resurrection word as the basis for steadfastness in labor (1 Corinthians 15:58); divine announcement grounds human endurance.

11.note: The watchman's reply — morning is coming, but so is the night — anticipates the night/day dualism of John 9:4 (work while it is day; night is coming) and the eschatological tension between the already of resurrection dawn and the still-coming darkness before the final day.

8.note: Death has been swallowed up in victory
He will wipe every tear from their eyes

6.note: Blessed are those invited to the wedding feast of the Lamb — the rich messianic banquet on Mount Zion for all peoples in Isaiah 25:6 is the OT archetype of the marriage supper of the Lamb; the choicest food and well-aged wine point forward to the eschatological feast Christ hosts in the new creation.

Blessed is everyone who will eat bread in the kingdom of God — the parable of the great banquet (Luke 14) is Jesus's reinterpretation of the Isaiah 25:6 feast; the invitation goes out to all peoples, and those who make excuses are shut out while the poor and lame are brought in.

7.note: When one turns to the Lord, the veil is removed — the covering cast over all peoples in Isaiah 25:7 (the veil spread over the nations) is the OT image Paul applies to the reading of Moses without Christ; the shroud that blinds is the type of the veil on the heart removed only by turning to the Lord in the Spirit.

11.note: Through foreign lips he will speak to this people

16.note: Anyone who trusts in him will never be put to shame

10.note: God gave them a spirit of stupor — eyes that do not see

13.note: These people honor me with their lips
These people honor me with their lips

14.note: I will destroy the wisdom of the wise

16.note: Should the potter be regarded like clay? — Paul develops the potter/clay analogy directly from this verse in Romans 9:20-21 to defend divine sovereignty in election; the creature challenging the Creator echoes Isaiah's rebuke of Israel's presumption.

18.note: In that day the deaf will hear and the blind will see — Jesus cites this exact cluster of messianic signs when answering John the Baptist's question (Matthew 11:5; Luke 7:22): the blind receive sight, the deaf hear, as proof that the kingdom has come.

19.note: The meek will find renewed joy and the poorest will exult in the Holy One — the beatitude Blessed are the meek (Matthew 5:5) and Blessed are the poor in spirit (Matthew 5:3) draw on this cluster of Isaiah's reversal promises; the kingdom belongs to those whom Isaiah describes here.

4.note: The sky rolls up like a scroll

8.note: For the LORD has a day of vengeance, a year of recompense for the cause of Zion — Revelation 19:2 echoes this pattern in the angel's declaration that God has judged Babylon the great and avenged the blood of his servants; the day of vengeance for Zion's cause (Isa 34) finds its ultimate fulfillment in the judgment of spiritual Babylon.

10.note: The smoke of Edom shall go up forever; from generation to generation it shall lie waste — Revelation 14:11 applies the same language of eternal smoke to the judgment of those who worship the beast; Isaiah's oracle against Edom (the type of all enemies of God's people) becomes Revelation's image for final eschatological judgment.

The smoke goes up forever and ever — Revelation 19:3 uses this precise language from Isaiah 34:10 to describe the fall of Babylon; the eternal smoking of Edom is the OT archetype for the finality of divine judgment.

3.note: The voice of one crying in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the LORD — all four Gospels cite Isaiah 40:3 as fulfilled in John the Baptist; the voice calling for the preparation of YHWH's way is the one who announces the arrival of Jesus, whose way John prepares

6.note: All flesh is like grass — the word endures forever
All flesh is like grass — the word of the Lord endures

13.note: Who has known the mind of the Lord?
We have the mind of Christ
Who has known the mind of the Lord?

1.note: Behold my servant whom I have chosen, my beloved in whom my soul delights — Matthew cites the first Servant Song (Isaiah 42:1-4) at length as fulfilled in Jesus's withdrawal from controversy and gentle healing ministry; the bruised reed he would not break is his treatment of the vulnerable
My servant whom I uphold — justice for the nations

6.note: A light for revelation to the Gentiles, and for glory to your people Israel — Simeon's nunc dimittis (Luke 2:32) is a direct citation of Isaiah 42:6 (I will give you as a covenant for the people, a light for the nations); the infant Jesus is identified as the fulfillment of the Servant's mission to be light for the Gentiles.

I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth — Paul and Barnabas directly cite Isaiah 42:6 (via Isaiah 49:6) to justify turning to the Gentiles; the Servant's commission to be a light to the nations is the scriptural mandate for the Gentile mission of the church.

7.note: He has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives and recovering of sight to the blind — Jesus's Nazareth manifesto (Luke 4:18) combines Isaiah 61:1 with Isaiah 42:7 (to open the eyes that are blind, to bring out the prisoners from the dungeon); Jesus presents himself as the Servant of Isa 42 fulfilling the mission of liberation.

9.note: Behold, the former things have come to pass, and new things I now declare — God's announcement of new things in Isaiah 42:9 anticipates the eschatological declaration of Revelation 21:5 (Behold, I am making all things new); what Isaiah declares as future prophecy is announced as accomplished reality in John's vision of the new creation.

10.note: Sing to the LORD a new song — the call to a new song in Isaiah 42:10 anticipates the heavenly throne room in Revelation 5:9 where the living creatures and elders sing a new song to the Lamb who was slain; the new song prompted by the LORD's triumph becomes the eternal new song of the Lamb's redemption in Revelation.

16.note: I will turn the darkness before them into light — the divine guide who turns darkness to light for the blind of Isaiah 42:16 is the figure Jesus identifies himself as in John 8:12 (I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will not walk in darkness but will have the light of life); the Servant of Isa 42 is the light.

6.note: I am the First and the Last -- Jesus claims the divine title

3.note: I will pour out my Spirit on your offspring — this Spirit-outpouring is directly fulfilled at Pentecost; Peter cites the parallel Joel 2:28 in Acts 2:17, but Isaiah 44:3 is the same promise from a different angle: water on thirsty ground, Spirit on descendants, the new-creation refreshment of dry souls.

Streams on dry ground / Spirit poured out — Jesus applies this to himself: whoever believes in me, rivers of living water will flow from within them (John 7:38-39), identifying the Spirit as the fulfillment of Isaiah's promise; the one who gives the Spirit is the one through whom the outpouring comes.

22.note: I have swept away your transgressions like a cloud, your sins like morning mist — Paul describes the same act through the cross: God cancelled the written code (the debt record) that stood against us and nailed it to the cross (Colossians 2:14); what Isaiah describes meteorologically, the atonement accomplishes forensically.

Transgressions swept away so Israel can return — the return enabled by sin-removal points to the cleansing power of Christ's blood: how much more will the blood of Christ, who offered himself unblemished to God, cleanse our consciences from dead works to serve the living God (Hebrews 9:14).

24.note: The LORD your Redeemer, who formed you in the womb, who made all things — the creator-Redeemer who stretches out the heavens alone and spreads out the earth is identified by John with the eternal Word through whom all things were made (John 1:3); the one who redeems is the same one who first created.

23.note: Every knee will bow, every tongue confess
At the name of Jesus every knee should bow
Every knee will bow before me, every tongue confess

9.note: Woe to him who quarrels with his Maker — will the clay say to the potter, What are you making? — Paul draws on this exact potter/clay language in Romans 9:20-21 (shall what is formed say to the one who formed it, Why did you make me like this?) to defend divine sovereignty in election; the Isaianic polemic against creaturely presumption becomes Paul's defense of God's freedom.

14.note: Truly God is with you, and there is no other — the nations' confession that God is present in his people finds its fulfillment in Immanuel: God with us (Matthew 1:23 citing Isaiah 7:14); in Christ, God's presence with his people reaches its irreversible climax.

17.note: Israel is saved by the LORD with an everlasting salvation — the eternal, inexhaustible nature of this salvation is realized in Christ: he became the source of eternal salvation for all who obey him (Hebrews 5:9); what Isaiah calls everlasting salvation is what Hebrews identifies as the salvation secured by the eternal Son through his once-for-all offering.

21.note: There is no God apart from me — a righteous God and a Savior — there is none besides me. The exclusive Savior claim of the one God points to Paul's declaration that there is one God and one mediator between God and humanity, Christ Jesus (1 Timothy 2:5); in Christ, the one-God of Isaiah is revealed as the one Savior of all.

22.note: Turn to me and be saved, all the ends of the earth — this universal invitation from the one God is the heartbeat of Revelation's final invitation: the Spirit and the bride say Come! Let the one who is thirsty come; let the one who wishes take the free gift of the water of life (Revelation 22:17); the call to all the ends of the earth is the Great Commission in prophetic form.

Turn to me and be saved, all the ends of the earth — Paul's gospel promise that everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved (Romans 10:13 citing Joel 2:32) operates within the theological frame Isaiah establishes here: the exclusive-God who is alone the Savior invites the entire world to turn and be saved.

7.note: I sit as queen and will never mourn -- plagues in one day

8.note: You who say in your heart, I am, and there is no one besides me — Babylon's self-deifying I am echoes the divine name and its claim to uniqueness (Isaiah 45:22; Isaiah 46:9); Revelation 18:7 explicitly cites this verse as the boast of spiritual Babylon: She says in her heart, I sit as a queen and I am no widow, and I will never see mourning — the same pride Isaiah deconstructs here.

I am, and there is no one besides me... I will be a mistress forever — Revelation 18:7 directly quotes the parallel verse 7 (I will be queen forever) in its portrait of Babylon/Rome: She says in her heart, I sit as queen, I am no widow, and will never see grief. John identifies spiritual Rome as the fulfillment of Babylon's boast; Isaiah's deconstruction is applied to the final empire.

9.note: Both of these — loss of children and widowhood — shall come upon you in a moment, in a single day — the sudden collapse of Babylon in a single day is the exact language of Revelation 18:8: therefore in one day her plagues will come — death, mourning, and famine — and she will be consumed by fire; John maps Isaiah's oracle against historical Babylon onto the eschatological fall of spiritual Babylon.

11.note: Evil shall come upon you suddenly, which you will not know how to conjure away; disaster shall fall upon you which you cannot atone for — the sudden unexpected collapse recalls Revelation 18:10: in one hour your judgment has come; the merchants of the earth weep over Babylon fallen in an hour, matching Isaiah's prediction of disaster that cannot be charmed away.

14.note: They are like straw; the fire devours them; they cannot save themselves from the power of the flame — the fire that devours the wise men of Babylon who cannot save themselves anticipates the lake of fire in Revelation 20:14-15, where no human wisdom or power provides escape; the impotence of Babylon's sorcerers before divine fire is the prototype of final judgment.

6.note: I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth — Paul and Barnabas cite Isaiah 49:6 as their scriptural warrant for turning to the Gentiles; the Servant's mission to be a light to the nations is the apostolic commission

A light for revelation to the Gentiles and for glory to your people Israel — Simeon's Nunc Dimittis applies Isaiah 49:6 to the infant Jesus; the Servant's mission as light-to-nations is announced over Jesus at the temple presentation

8.note: In the time of my favor I heard you

7.note: How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news
How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news

11.note: Come out from them and be separate

15.note: Those who were never told about him will see, and those who have never heard will understand — Paul cites Isaiah 52:15 as his apostolic principle for pioneer mission: to proclaim where Christ has not been named, fulfilling the Servant's revelation to nations who never heard
Those who were not told about him will see

1.note: Who has believed what he heard from us? — John cites Isaiah 53:1 to explain Israel's unbelief despite Jesus's signs; the question of Isa 53 (who believed the Servant-report?) is the question that frames the rejection of Jesus
Lord, who has believed what he heard from us? — Paul cites Isaiah 53:1 to explain Israel's failure to embrace the gospel; the Servant's rejected message is the gospel that Israel stumbles over
Lord, who has believed our report?

3.note: He came to his own and was rejected

4.note: He took our illnesses and bore our diseases — Matthew cites Isaiah 53:4 in connection with Jesus's healing ministry; the Servant's bearing of human disease and suffering is enacted in Jesus's healings, not only in his atoning death
By his wounds we are healed

5.note: Pierced for our transgressions — Christ died for the ungodly

6.note: We all like sheep have gone astray

7.note: He was led like a sheep to the slaughter and like a lamb before its shearer is silent — Philip reads Isaiah 53:7-8 to the Ethiopian eunuch and proclaims Jesus as the fulfillment; the silent suffering of the Servant is Jesus's silent submission to his accusers and executioners
He did not open his mouth — before Pilate

9.note: Buried in a rich man's tomb
No deceit found in his mouth
No sin was found in his mouth — no deceit

12.note: And he was numbered with the transgressors — Jesus at the Last Supper applies Isaiah 53:12 to himself: this must be fulfilled in me; the Servant's identification with sinners is Jesus's crucifixion between two criminals

1.note: Rejoice, barren woman — children of promise

3.note: Holy blessings promised to David -- fulfilled in the resurrection

7.note: My house shall be called a house of prayer
My house shall be called a house of prayer
My house shall be called a house of prayer
Their feet are swift to shed blood

20.note: The deliverer will come from Zion
The deliverer will come from Zion and remove ungodliness

1.note: Light for the Gentiles — Simeon: a light of revelation to the Gentiles

The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor — Jesus reads Isaiah 61:1-2 in the Nazareth synagogue and says: Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing; his entire ministry is the proclamation of the Jubilee-anointing of Isa 61
Rider in blood-stained robe -- winepress of God's wrath

4.note: No eye has seen, no ear heard what God has prepared

1.note: Found by those who did not seek me
Found by those who did not seek me

2.note: All day I held out my hands to a disobedient people
All day long I held out my hands to a disobedient people

17.note: I create new heavens and a new earth — Paul's new creation language in 2 Corinthians 5:17 (if anyone is in Christ, new creation) draws from Isaiah 65:17's eschatological promise; the new creation is already present in Christ

Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth — Revelation's new creation vision fulfills Isaiah 65:17 and 66:22 literally; John sees the eschatological reality that Isaiah prophesied in the vision of the New Jerusalem
New heavens and a new earth -- Peter points to Isaiah's promise
New heavens and a new earth -- John sees the new creation

1.note: Heaven is my throne -- Stephen: God does not live in houses made by hands

7.note: Woman giving birth before labor -- nation born at once

15.note: Flaming fire punishing those who do not know God

2.note: You have come to Mount Zion, to the city of the living God — the author of Hebrews announces the eschatological establishment of the mountain of Isaiah 2:2 as already accessible through Christ; the latter-days mountain-establishment is the new covenant Zion

The great, high mountain and the holy city Jerusalem — John's vision of the New Jerusalem on the great mountain fulfills Isaiah 2:2's promise that the mountain of the LORD's house would be established as the highest; all nations now flow to it through the Lamb

3.note: Worship neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem — Jesus reframes the Isaiah 2:3 pilgrimage to Zion; the true worshipers now come to the Father in spirit and truth through Christ rather than to geographic Zion

Make disciples of all nations — the great commission is the gospel fulfillment of the nations streaming to Zion in Isaiah 2:3; instead of nations going up to Jerusalem, the disciples go out to all nations with the word of the LORD

4.note: No more death, mourning, crying, or pain — the swords-into-plowshares peace of Isaiah 2:4 is the new creation reality of Rev 21; the eschatological end of war achieved through the Lamb's victory

God has fixed a day to judge the world by the man he has appointed — Isaiah 2:4's promise that the LORD shall judge between nations is fulfilled by the appointed judge, Jesus Christ, raised from the dead as guarantee of the coming judgment

10.note: Kings of the earth and the great ones hiding in caves — Revelation 6:15-17 is the direct NT echo of Isaiah 2:10,19,21; those hiding from the LORD's terror in Isa 2 are the exact population of Rev 6 hiding from the face of the Lamb on the great day of wrath

19.note: Calling on mountains and rocks to fall on them and hide them from the face of him who sits on the throne — a direct allusion to Isaiah 2:19-21; John identifies the one whose terror drives people to the caves as the Lamb, confirming Christ as the LORD of Isa 2

14.note: The parable of the wicked tenants who plundered the vineyard — Isaiah 3:14 indicts Israel's leaders for devouring the vineyard; Jesus's parable of the wicked tenants (Matthew 21:33-43; Luke 20:9-19) applies the same indictment, adding that the owner's Son is killed, and the vineyard given to others

The kingdom of God will be taken away and given to a people producing its fruits — the transfer of the vineyard from those who plundered it (Isaiah 3:14) to a faithful nation; Christ fulfills and surpasses the Isa 3 indictment

2.note: The sunrise (anatole) shall visit us from on high — Zechariah's canticle uses the same Greek word for 'branch/sprout' (anatole/tsemach) that the LXX uses for Isaiah 4:2's Branch of the LORD; Christ is the messianic Branch who is beautiful and glorious

I am the root and the offspring of David, the bright morning star — Christ the Branch who springs from David; Isaiah 4:2's Branch of the LORD that is glorious for the survivors of Israel is the risen Christ revealed as the radiant source of the new creation

4.note: He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire — John the Baptist explicitly connects his baptism with the spirit of judgment and burning in Isaiah 4:4; the Lord's cleansing of the daughters of Zion by a spirit of burning is the Pentecost poured out by the risen Christ

Divided tongues as of fire appeared and rested on each of them — the Pentecost fire is the fulfillment of Isaiah 4:4's spirit of burning; the same purifying fire that Isaiah foresaw for Zion descends on the new covenant community

5.note: He who sits on the throne will shelter them with his presence — the divine tabernacling over the redeemed echoes Isaiah 4:5-6's cloud and fire over Mount Zion; the Lamb's canopy replaces the wilderness pillar as the permanent shelter of God's people in the new creation

The Word became flesh and tabernacled (eskenosen) among us — the divine cloud-and-fire presence of Isaiah 4:5 that tabernacled over Zion becomes in John's Gospel the incarnate Word dwelling among his people as God's permanent presence

2.note: The great multitude crying 'Salvation belongs to our God' — the eschatological fulfillment of Isaiah 12:2's 'God is my salvation'; the redeemed from every nation sing the same salvation cry as the restored exiles, now directed to God and the Lamb

With the heart one believes and is justified, and with the mouth one confesses and is saved — Paul's salvation formula echoes Isaiah 12:2's personal declaration 'God is my salvation'; the individual confession 'the LORD has become my salvation' is the shape of saving faith

3.note: Whoever believes in me, as the Scripture has said, out of his heart will flow rivers of living water — Jesus cites Scripture (likely Isaiah 12:3 / Isaiah 55:1) to identify himself as the source of the wells of salvation; the water drawn with joy from the wells of salvation is the Spirit flowing from the risen Christ

The water that I will give will become a spring of water welling up to eternal life — the Samaritan woman and the well of salvation; Isaiah 12:3's wells of salvation find their personal embodiment in Christ who gives water the drinker never thirsts for again

4.note: Everyone who calls upon the name of the Lord shall be saved — Peter's Pentecost sermon (citing Joel 2:32) announces the fulfillment of Isaiah 12:4's 'call upon his name'; the name proclaimed in all the earth is the name of Jesus Christ, the exalted Lord

At the name of Jesus every knee should bow — the exaltation of the name fulfills Isaiah 12:4's 'proclaim that his name is exalted'; the name made known among the peoples is specifically the name given to the crucified and risen Christ

6.note: The dwelling place of God is with man — the Holy One great in Israel's midst in Isaiah 12:6 finds its eschatological completion in the new creation where God tabernacles permanently with his people; the Holy One who was great in Zion is the Lamb in the midst of the holy city

You have come to Mount Zion, to the city of the living God, to the heavenly Jerusalem — the inhabitant of Zion who shouts for joy at the Holy One's presence (Isaiah 12:6) is the church that has already come to the heavenly Zion through Christ

12.note: I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven — Jesus responds to the disciples' report of demonic defeat by citing Isaiah 14:12 as the paradigm; the fall of the Day Star (Helel/Lucifer) from heaven is the archetype that Christ witnesses in Satan's defeat through his ministry

The great dragon was thrown down, that ancient serpent called the devil and Satan — the cosmic fall of Isaiah 14:12 is the heavenly prototype that Revelation depicts eschatologically; the king of Babylon's fall mirrors and typifies the final expulsion of the adversary

13.note: The man of lawlessness who opposes and exalts himself above every god — Paul's description of the anti-Christ echoes the pride of Isaiah 14:13-14 ('I will ascend above the heights of the clouds; I will make myself like the Most High'); the Babylonian king's hubris is the type of every anti-God power

15.note: The devil was thrown into the lake of fire — the casting of the Day Star into Sheol/the pit (Isaiah 14:15) is the OT prototype for the final imprisonment of Satan; the pit of Isa 14 is the lake of fire of Rev 20

27.note: For those who love God all things work together for good — Paul's confidence in God's purpose echoes Isaiah 14:24-27's declaration that God's counsel stands and his purpose cannot be thwarted; the unstoppable purpose of God (Isaiah 14:27) grounds the assurance of Rom 8

1.note: Alas, alas, you great city Babylon! For in a single hour your judgment has come — the overnight destruction of Ar of Moab in Isaiah 15:1 is the OT type of the sudden, catastrophic fall of Babylon in Revelation 18; both depict the swiftness of divine judgment on the proud city-civilization

5.note: The Lord God will give him the throne of his father David — Gabriel's annunciation fulfills Isaiah 16:5's promise of a throne established in steadfast love in the tent of David; the one who will sit in faithfulness and judge is Jesus Christ

I will return and rebuild the tent of David that has fallen — James cites Amos 9:11 at the Jerusalem council, but the rebuilt Davidic tent of Isaiah 16:5 is the same promise; the throne of David is restored in the risen Christ who judges with justice

12.note: The waters you saw are peoples and multitudes and nations and languages — Revelation explicitly interprets the roaring-waters imagery (already present in Isaiah 17:12-13) as the hostile nations; the thunder of many peoples in Isa 17 is the prototype of the raging sea of nations in Revelation

I heard what seemed to be the voice of a great multitude, like the roar of many waters — the redeemed multitude's hallelujah roars like the threatening nations of Isaiah 17:12-13, but the direction is reversed; the peoples who roared against God now roar his praise in the new creation

7.note: The kings of the earth will bring their glory into the New Jerusalem — Isaiah 18:7's vision of gifts being brought from the people of Cush to Mount Zion is the OT prototype of the eschatological pilgrimage of the nations; what begins as tribute to YHWH's Zion ends as the nations streaming into the heavenly Jerusalem with their glory

1.note: Behold, he is coming with the clouds — the LORD riding on a swift cloud to judge Egypt (Isaiah 19:1) is the OT prototype of divine theophany on clouds; Revelation applies the cloud-coming to Christ's return, confirming that the divine cloud-rider of Isa 19 is the coming Christ

24.note: He has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility — Paul's vision of former enemies (Jew and Gentile) made one in Christ fulfills Isaiah 19:24-25's astonishing oracle that Egypt and Assyria will be blessed alongside Israel as equal partners in God's purposes; the enmity between nations dissolved in Christ

There is neither Jew nor Greek, for you are all one in Christ Jesus — the radical inclusion of Isaiah 19:25 ('Blessed be Egypt my people, and Assyria the work of my hands, and Israel my inheritance') is the OT root of Paul's declaration that ethnic distinctions no longer define covenant membership

16.note: Egypt trembling before the hand of the LORD anticipates the birth-pangs language of eschatological shaking (Matthew 24:6-8); the nations in terror before divine judgment is a recurrent apocalyptic pattern.

18.note: Five Egyptian cities swearing loyalty to the LORD and speaking the language of Canaan anticipates Zephaniah 3:9 (a pure language given to the nations to call on the LORD) and the Pentecost reversal of Babel (Acts 2:4-11).

19.note: An altar to the LORD in the heart of Egypt anticipates the new-covenant worship Jesus announces to the Samaritan woman: worship in spirit and truth, not limited to one geographic location (John 4:21-23); fulfilled in the global church.

20.note: The sign/witness to the LORD in Egypt foreshadows the Gentile mission; Paul's catena in Romans 15:9-12 builds on Isaiah's vision of nations calling on the LORD's name, of which this passage is an early anticipation.

21.note: Egypt giving glory to God (the sign/witness in the land) anticipates the nations giving glory to God in Revelation 11:13, the post-judgment response that signals eschatological conversion.

22.note: The LORD striking Egypt and then healing — turning punishment into restoration — parallels the NT pattern of discipline leading to healing (James 5:15; Hebrews 12:10-11); judgment is not the final word.

23.note: A highway uniting Egypt, Assyria, and Israel — three formerly hostile nations — as co-worshippers of the LORD prefigures the breaking down of the dividing wall in Christ (Ephesians 2:14); the triple blessing anticipates the one new humanity.

25.note: Blessed be Egypt my people, and Assyria the work of my hands — extending covenant language (my people, the work of my hands) to Israel's ancient enemies anticipates the Gentile adoption Paul describes using Hosea in Romans 9:25-26 and the nations becoming heirs of Abraham (Galatians 3:14; Ephesians 2:12-13).

3.note: Agabus took Paul's belt and bound his own hands and feet — the NT prophetic sign-act follows the OT pattern of Isaiah walking naked for three years as a sign (Isaiah 20:3-4); Agabus performs a similar enacted prophecy, demonstrating continuity of the prophetic sign-act tradition now fulfilled in the Spirit-inspired church

2.note: Isaiah's commanded nakedness as a three-year prophetic sign-act is one of the most radical embodied prophecies in the OT; the shame-bearing prophet anticipates the shame endured by the servants listed in Hebrews 11:37 and ultimately by Christ (Hebrews 12:2).

13.note: If the dead are not raised, 'Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die' — Paul directly quotes Isaiah 22:13 as the logical conclusion of denying resurrection; the fatalistic banquet-motto of Jerusalem under siege becomes the reductio ad absurdum of the Corinthians' denial of bodily resurrection

22.note: The words of the holy one, the true one, who has the key of David, who opens and no one will shut, who shuts and no one opens — Christ directly applies Isaiah 22:22 to himself in the letter to Philadelphia; the key of David given to Eliakim in Isa 22 is the messianic authority given to Christ over God's household

20.note: Eliakim is called my servant — a Servant of the LORD title also given to the Davidic king; his appointment over the household foreshadows the faithful servant of the parables (Matthew 24:45; Luke 16:10) and ultimately Christ as the one appointed over the Father's house (Hebrews 3:6).

23.note: Eliakim driven like a peg into a solid place, becoming a throne of glory — the firm peg as anchor of trust anticipates Hebrews 6:19 (hope as an anchor, firm and secure, entering the inner sanctuary); the image of something firmly placed inside the holy place of God's presence.

17.note: The kings of the earth will bring their glory into the New Jerusalem — Isaiah 23:17-18's vision that Tyre's commercial wealth will eventually be 'holy to the LORD' is the OT prototype of the eschatological redeemption of the nations' wealth; what was plunder becomes consecrated tribute in the new creation

The merchants of the earth weep and mourn for Babylon — Revelation's portrait of Babylon as the anti-Tyre draws on Isa 23's oracle; the merchants who weep over Babylon (Rev 18) are the inverse of the nations whose trade is consecrated to God in Isaiah 23:18

1.note: The lament over Tyre — no harbor, ships of Tarshish wailing — is absorbed into Revelation 18:17-19's lament over fallen Babylon; every merchant ship captain who trades on the sea mourns the great city, echoing Isaiah's lamentation over the merchant city of Tyre.

8.note: Tyre the crown-bestowing city whose traders were princes — the pride of merchant commerce — anticipates Revelation 18:23 where Babylon's merchants were the great ones of the earth; the critique of commercial empire as idolatry runs from Isaiah through Revelation.

15.note: Seventy years of Tyre's forgetting echoes the seventy-year exile of Jeremiah 25:11-12 and Daniel's seventy weeks (Daniel 9:2,24-27); the number seventy as a bounded period of judgment followed by restoration is a structuring motif across the prophets.

18.note: Tyre's merchandise and earnings set apart as holy to the LORD — not stored or hoarded but given for abundant provision — anticipates Revelation 21:24-26 where the nations bring their glory and honor into the new Jerusalem; the wealth of the Gentiles consecrated to God's service.

1.note: The earth made empty and waste, turned upside down — the cosmic upheaval of Isa 24 (the Little Apocalypse) is the OT prototype for Rev 6's seal judgments; the heavens rolled up and earth shaken share the same apocalyptic grammar.

Immediately after the tribulation, the sun darkened and moon not give its light — Jesus draws on the cosmic-catastrophe imagery of Isa 24 for his eschatological discourse; the darkening of luminaries is the OT apocalyptic language Jesus inherits and reapplies to his own return.

17.note: People fainting with fear and foreboding of what is coming on the world — Jesus's eschatological discourse in Luke 21 uses the same inescapable terror logic of Isaiah 24:17-18 (pit and snare and net); whoever flees the pit falls into the snare, the inescapability of divine judgment characterizes both passages.

21.note: The LORD will punish the host of heaven and the kings of the earth — the binding and imprisonment of cosmic powers in Isaiah 24:21-22 (shut up in prison for many days) is the OT prototype for Revelation 20:2-3's binding of Satan for a thousand years; the pattern of cosmic arrest followed by final visitation is identical.

Angels who did not keep their own position kept in eternal chains in gloomy darkness until the judgment — the imprisonment of the host of heaven in Isaiah 24:22 (gathered in a pit and shut up) is the background for Jude's reference to the fallen watchers awaiting their sentence.

23.note: The new Jerusalem has no need of sun or moon because the glory of God gives it light — Revelation 21:23 fulfills Isaiah 24:23, where the LORD reigns on Mount Zion in glory so blazing that moon and sun are abashed; the LORD's direct presence replaces created luminaries in both visions.

11.note: A fearful expectation of judgment and a fury of fire that will consume the adversaries — the fire consuming God's enemies in Isaiah 26:11 is the conceptual background for Hebrews 10:27's warning about what awaits those who willfully reject Christ after receiving the knowledge of the truth.

19.note: The hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out — Jesus's resurrection declaration draws on Isaiah 26:19 (your dead shall live; their bodies shall rise); the dew of light as the agent of resurrection points forward to Christ, who is himself the resurrection and the life.

The dead in Christ will rise first — the general resurrection motif of Isaiah 26:19 (awake and sing, you who dwell in the dust) is the OT ground of Paul's resurrection confidence; the awakening from the dust is inaugurated in Christ's resurrection and will be completed at his parousia.

20.note: Yet a little while, and the coming one will come and will not delay — the author of Hebrews directly adapts Isaiah 26:20 (hide yourself for a little while until the fury has passed) alongside Habakkuk 2:3-4; the sheltering until judgment passes becomes the NT posture of patient faith awaiting the returning Christ.

9.note: This will be my covenant with them when I take away their sins — Paul directly quotes Isaiah 27:9 in his climactic conclusion to the olive-tree discourse (Romans 11:26-27); the removal of Jacob's sin by covenant is Paul's OT scriptural ground for Israel's future salvation through the Deliverer who comes from Zion.

13.note: He will send out his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds — the great trumpet of Isaiah 27:13 gathering dispersed Israel from Assyria and Egypt is the OT prototype for Jesus's eschatological gathering of the elect; the one great trumpet signals the end of exile and the ingathering of God's people.

The Lord will descend with a loud command and with the trumpet of God — the gathering trumpet of Isaiah 27:13 is the eschatological instrument of Christ's parousia; what began as a trumpet call for scattered Israel becomes in Paul the trumpet announcing the resurrection of the dead and the meeting with the Lord.

8.note: Write it before them on a tablet, inscribe it in a book as a witness forever — the command to inscribe prophetic testimony permanently anticipates the Apocalypse's opening command (Revelation 1:11: write what you see in a book); prophecy preserved in writing as enduring witness.

9.note: A rebellious people, deceptive children who refuse to hear the instruction of the LORD — Stephen's indictment in Acts 7:51 (You stiff-necked people, you always resist the Holy Spirit) echoes this Isaianic diagnosis of Israel's chronic resistance to prophetic instruction.

15.note: In repentance and rest you shall be saved; in quietness and trust is your strength — Jesus draws on this when he says Come to me all who are weary and I will give you rest (Matthew 11:28-29); the Isaianic rest of repentant trust becomes the rest Christ offers to all who come to him.

The rest promised through quietness and trust anticipates Hebrews' theological development of Sabbath-rest (Hebrews 4:9-11); those who believe enter that rest, the sabbath-rest that remains for the people of God.

18.note: The LORD waits to be gracious to you and rises up to show mercy — the divine patience that waits for repentance is the same quality Peter cites in 2 Peter 3:9 (the Lord is not slow; he is patient, not wishing any to perish but for all to come to repentance).

19.note: He will surely be gracious at the sound of your cry; as soon as he hears he will answer — Jesus draws on the pattern of the God who hears and answers the cries of his people when he assures his disciples that the Father will answer those who cry to him day and night (Luke 18:7-8).

21.note: Your ears will hear a voice behind you saying, This is the way, walk in it — the guiding voice of the shepherd is the theme of John 10:4 (the sheep follow him because they know his voice); the LORD's guiding voice is fulfilled in Christ who is the way (John 14:6).

This is the way — the divine voice pointing to the right path is fulfilled in Christ's I am the way (John 14:6); what Isaiah describes as the word of the LORD behind you guiding your steps is incarnated in the one who is himself the way.

26.note: The moon will be like the sun and the sun seven times brighter — the intensified light of the eschatological age anticipates Revelation 21:23 where the city has no need of sun or moon for the Lamb is its light; Isaiah's hyperbole becomes the new creation's reality.

27.note: The name of the LORD comes from afar, burning with anger, with a devouring fire — the divine warrior imagery of the coming judgment (Revelation 19:11-15) draws on this Isaianic portrait of the LORD arriving in fiery wrath to execute judgment on the nations.

33.note: Topheth has long been prepared for the king — the Valley of Hinnom/Topheth as the place of fire and judgment is the background for Jesus' warning about Gehenna (the fire prepared for the devil, Matthew 25:41); Topheth becomes the prototype of the final judgment's fire.

3.note: The Egyptians are human and not God; their horses are flesh and not spirit — the flesh/spirit contrast that defines the inadequacy of human power is the same contrast Jesus uses with Nicodemus (John 3:6: what is born of flesh is flesh, born of Spirit is spirit); only divine power saves.

Flesh-versus-spirit in military terms: relying on horses (flesh) versus the LORD (spirit) anticipates Paul's declaration that our weapons are not of the flesh but have divine power (2 Corinthians 10:4); the spiritual warfare Paul describes is grounded in this Isaianic contrast.

5.note: Like birds hovering over their young, so the LORD will shield Jerusalem — Jesus applies this very imagery to himself: How often I wanted to gather your children as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings (Matthew 23:37 / Luke 13:34), identifying himself as the one who longs to do what Isaiah describes the LORD doing.

8.note: The Assyrian will fall by a sword that is not of any man — the defeat of the enemy by divine rather than human agency points forward to Revelation 19:15 (from Christ's mouth comes a sharp sword with which to strike down the nations); the sword that is not human is the word of God.

9.note: His stronghold will crumble in terror at the LORD's signal — the crumbling stronghold of the enemy anticipates Christ's declaration that the gates of Hades will not prevail against his church (Matthew 16:18); the LORD's signal is the resurrection signal that defeats death's stronghold.

1.note: A king will reign in righteousness and rulers will govern with justice — the righteous king is the messianic promise (Jeremiah 23:5: the righteous Branch) fulfilled in Christ; Luke 1:32-33 applies it directly: the LORD God will give him the throne of David and his kingdom will have no end.

The king reigning in righteousness anticipates Revelation 19:11 (the rider on the white horse, called Faithful and True, who in righteousness judges and makes war); the Isaianic vision of the righteous king finds its eschatological fulfillment here.

2.note: Like streams of water in a dry land, like the shade of a great rock in a weary land — Jesus is both the rock (1 Corinthians 10:4) and the source of living water (John 4:14; Isaiah 7:38); the shelter and refreshment Isaiah describes for the righteous king's reign is fulfilled in Christ.

3.note: The eyes of those who see will no longer be closed, the ears of those who hear will listen — this reversal of the blindness/deafness of Isaiah 6:9-10 finds partial fulfillment in Jesus' words to the disciples: Blessed are your eyes because they see, your ears because they hear (Matthew 13:16); full restoration comes in the kingdom.

15.note: Until the Spirit is poured upon us from on high, and the wilderness becomes a fruitful orchard — the Pentecost event is the fulfillment of this outpouring; Peter cites the parallel passage (Joel 2:28-29) in Acts 2:17 (I will pour out my Spirit on all people), and Isaiah 32:15 is the same promise from a different angle.

The Spirit poured out from on high transforming the wilderness anticipates Jesus' promise of rivers of living water (John 7:38-39); John explicitly identifies this with the Spirit, whom those who believed would receive after Jesus was glorified.

17.note: The fruit of righteousness will be peace, and the result of righteousness will be quietness and confidence — James 3:18 (Peacemakers who sow in peace raise a harvest of righteousness) draws on this Isaianic connection between righteousness and peace as fruit; the Spirit-renewed community bears this fruit.

The result of righteousness will be quietness and confidence forever — Paul's promise that the peace of God which transcends all understanding will guard your hearts (Philippians 4:7) echoes this Isaianic vision of the settled quietness that righteousness produces in the Spirit-renewed age.

18.note: My people will dwell in peaceful homes, in secure dwellings, in undisturbed resting places — the shalom dwelling of the people of God anticipates Revelation 21:3-4 (God's dwelling place is now among the people; he will wipe every tear); Isaiah's promise of the secure dwelling finds its eschatological fulfillment in the new creation.

20.note: Blessed are you who sow beside every stream — the beatitude of generous, well-watered sowing anticipates the parable of the sower (Matthew 13:23: the one who hears the word and understands it produces a crop); and 2 Corinthians 9:6 (whoever sows generously will reap generously).

6.note: In Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge — Isaiah 33:6's promise that wisdom and knowledge shall be the stability of the times, the fear of the LORD his treasure, anticipates Paul's declaration that all such treasure is hidden in Christ; the covenantal stability Israel sought in wisdom is now located in the person of the Son.

14.note: Our God is a consuming fire — Hebrews 12:29 quotes Deuteronomy 4:24 but the rhetorical question of Isaiah 33:14 (who among us can dwell with the consuming fire? who can live with everlasting burning?) provides the same logic; both passages press toward the same answer: only those whose sins are covered by Christ's blood can stand before the holy fire of God.

15.note: Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God — the righteous man of Isaiah 33:15-16 (who walks righteously, speaks uprightly, stops his ears from hearing of bloodshed) shall dwell on the heights and see the king in his beauty (v17); Jesus's Beatitude is the new-covenant distillation of the Isaianic portrait of the one who dwells with God.

17.note: They will see his face — the promise that the righteous shall see the king in his beauty (Isaiah 33:17) is the OT anticipation of Revelation 22:4's crowning promise that his servants shall see his face; the vision of the enthroned king that was conditional in Isa 33 is given freely to all the redeemed in the new Jerusalem.

20.note: The city of our appointed feasts — Isaiah 33:20's vision of Jerusalem as the unmoving tent, the quiet habitation, points forward to Hebrews 11:10's city with foundations whose designer and builder is God; the earthly Zion that could not be shaken (Isaiah 33:20 — its stakes will never be pulled up) is fulfilled in the unshakeable heavenly city.

I saw the holy city, the new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven — the quiet habitation of Isaiah 33:20, the tabernacle of God with his people where no galley ship can come, is the OT anticipation of the new Jerusalem that descends as the eternal dwelling; both texts describe the permanent, unassailable city of God's presence.

22.note: There is only one lawgiver and judge, he who is able to save and to destroy — James directly echoes Isaiah 33:22 (the LORD is our judge, the LORD is our lawgiver, the LORD is our king; he will save us); both texts insist on a single divine sovereign who combines legislative, judicial, and saving authority, which is fulfilled in Christ (John 5:22; Acts 5:31).

24.note: No inhabitant will say I am sick; the people who dwell there will be forgiven their iniquity — the eschatological Zion of Isaiah 33:24 links physical healing with the forgiveness of sin; Jesus's healing of the paralytic (Mark 2:5-10) makes the same connection, claiming authority to forgive sins as the validation of messianic healing power.

1.note: The wilderness and the dry land shall be glad; the desert shall rejoice and blossom like a crocus — the blooming desert of Isa 35 is the OT vision of the new creation that Rev 22 fulfills in the river of life flowing from the throne through a verdant garden-city; what was barren becomes abundantly fruitful through God's direct presence.

3.note: Strengthen the weak hands and make firm the feeble knees — Hebrews 12:12 directly quotes Isaiah 35:3, applying it to the endurance of Christians under trial; the exhortation to strengthen what is drooping is fulfilled in the community of believers who run the race set before them by looking to Jesus.

5.note: The blind receive their sight and the lame walk, lepers are cleansed and the deaf hear — Jesus directly cites Isaiah 35:5-6 in his answer to John the Baptist's question (Matthew 11:4-5; Luke 7:22): 'Go and tell John what you hear and see.' This is Jesus's definitive self-identification as the fulfillment of Isa 35; the signs of healing are the messianic credentials prophesied here.

Jesus answered them: Go and tell John what you have seen and heard — the blind see, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised (Luke 7:22) is a direct citation of Isaiah 35:5-6 expanded with Isaiah 61:1; Jesus's healing ministry is not just compassion but prophetic sign-fulfillment of Isaiah's messianic portrait.

6.note: Waters break forth in the wilderness and streams in the desert — the water springing up in the dry land of Isaiah 35:6-7 is the OT type of the living water Jesus promises: rivers of living water flowing from the believer (John 7:38), which John interprets as the Spirit; both Isaiah and Jesus use water-in-the-desert as the sign of eschatological life.

8.note: A highway shall be there, and it shall be called the Way of Holiness — the Way of Holiness in Isaiah 35:8 (on which the unclean shall not pass; it is for those who walk on the way) points directly to Jesus's declaration 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life' (John 14:6); Jesus is himself the Way of Holiness on which God's pilgrim people travel.

10.note: The ransomed of the LORD shall return and come to Zion with singing; everlasting joy shall be upon their heads; sorrow and sighing shall flee away — Revelation 21:4 directly fulfills Isaiah 35:10: God will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain; what Isa 35 promises for the restored exiles is given in its fullness to the redeemed of every nation in the new creation.

6.note: A broken reed is Egypt — the Rabshakeh's taunt that trusting Egypt is like leaning on a broken reed that will pierce the hand anticipates the NT critique of trusting in human strength; Paul's 'not trusting in ourselves but in God who raises the dead' (2 Corinthians 1:9) is the theological resolution to Hezekiah's predicament.

15.note: Do not let Hezekiah make you trust in the LORD — the Rabshakeh's mockery of trust in God becomes, by contrast, the very posture the NT requires; Romans 10:11 cites Isaiah 28:16 (no one who believes in him will be put to shame), and Hezekiah's preserved trust in the LORD that is vindicated in Isaiah 37 is the OT type of saving faith.

18.note: Has any of the gods of the nations delivered his land out of the hand of the king of Assyria? — the Rabshakeh's boast that no national god could resist Assyria is the OT type of the beast's claim to sovereignty; Revelation 17:14's response — the Lamb will conquer them — is the ultimate answer to this taunt, demonstrating that the LORD whom Hezekiah trusted is the King of kings.

16.note: O LORD of hosts, God of Israel, you alone are the God of all kingdoms of the earth; you have made heaven and earth — Hezekiah's prayer of absolute monotheism and Creator-sovereignty echoes in Revelation 4:11 (Worthy are you, our Lord and God, to receive glory and honor and power, for you created all things); the throne-room doxology in Revelation is the eschatological fulfillment of the sovereignty claim Hezekiah made in prayer.

The God who made the world and everything in it, being Lord of heaven and earth — Paul's Areopagus speech echoes Hezekiah's confession (Isaiah 37:16) that the LORD made heaven and earth; the monotheistic Creator-claim is both the ground of Hezekiah's prayer and the premise of Paul's proclamation to the nations.

19.note: An idol is nothing at all in the world, and there is no God but one — Paul's declaration directly reflects the OT logic of Isaiah 37:19 (the gods of the nations are no gods; they are the work of human hands, wood and stone); Hezekiah's prayer articulates the axiomatic distinction between the living God and manufactured idols that Paul applies to meat offered to idols.

31.note: The surviving remnant of the house of Judah shall again take root downward and bear fruit upward — the organic metaphor of the remnant rooted and fruitful anticipates Jesus's vine-and-branches teaching (John 15:5); the fruitbearing comes from remaining attached to the living source, just as Judah's survival comes from being rooted in the LORD.

36.note: The angel of the LORD went out and struck down 185,000 in the Assyrian camp — a single divine agent annihilating an entire army overnight is the most dramatic type in the OT of the eschatological defeat of God's enemies; Revelation 19:11-21 depicts the rider on the white horse striking down the nations, the final antitype of this event.

10.note: I said in the middle of my days I must depart; I am consigned to the gates of Sheol for the rest of my years — Hezekiah's descent to the gates of death is the OT personal prototype of the mortal crisis Christ entered through crucifixion; but where Hezekiah merely approached the gates, Christ descended into death and rose with the keys of Death and Hades (Revelation 1:18), so no one who trusts him need pass through those gates permanently.

11.note: I shall look on the LORD no more in the land of the living — Hezekiah's lament that death separates from the covenant community of worship anticipates Psalms 116:9 (I will walk before the LORD in the land of the living) and the resurrection hope; the NT answers Hezekiah's despair by declaring that in Christ there is no final separation from the presence of God.

17.note: You have cast all my sins behind your back — the complete, backward-casting forgiveness Hezekiah received anticipates the complete cancellation of the debt record that Colossians 2:14 describes: God has forgiven our trespasses, canceling the record of debt that stood against us by nailing it to the cross; both texts describe divine forgiveness as a definitive, irreversible act.

Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness — the forgiveness Hezekiah experienced (you have cast all my sins behind your back, v17) required a ground; Hebrews 9:22 locates that ground in blood-atonement, which in the OT was the sacrificial system and in the NT is the blood of Christ; Hezekiah's forgiveness was a promissory note drawn on Christ's sacrifice.

18.note: Sheol does not thank you; death does not praise you; those who go down to the pit do not hope for your faithfulness — this lament (that the dead cannot praise God) is the precise problem that Christ's resurrection answers; 1 Corinthians 15:55 (O death, where is your victory?) is the triumphant reversal of Hezekiah's canticle, enabled by the resurrection of Christ.

The dead cannot praise God — the silence of Sheol in Isaiah 38:18 is shattered by the endless praise of the Lamb in Revelation 5:9-14; every creature in heaven, on earth, and under the earth joins the new song, the eschatological answer to Hezekiah's lament that death ends worship.

20.note: The LORD will save me, and we will sing my songs with stringed instruments all the days of our lives at the house of the LORD — Hezekiah's vow to sing praise in the temple all his days is the prototype of the eternal praise in the heavenly sanctuary; Revelation 15:3 depicts the victorious singing the song of Moses and the Lamb in the temple of God — the ultimate fulfillment of this vow.

6.note: Behold, the days are coming when all that is in your house shall be carried to Babylon — Isaiah's announcement to Hezekiah that the Babylonian exile will come follows the same prophetic pattern Jesus applies to Jerusalem's destruction by Rome (Luke 21:24: Jerusalem will be trampled underfoot by the Gentiles); both events are divine judgment following Israel's unfaithfulness, and both are announced by a prophet to a generation that will not personally suffer them.

I will send you into exile beyond Babylon — Stephen's speech (Acts 7:43) cites the Babylon exile as the culmination of Israel's long pattern of rejecting God's word; Isaiah 39:6 is the prophetic announcement of that exile, and both texts use Babylon as the place of divine judgment for covenant unfaithfulness.

7.note: Some of your own sons who will come from you shall be taken away and shall be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon — the deportation of Judah's sons to Babylon is the historical event that Matthew 1:11-12 marks as a turning point in Jesus's genealogy (the deportation to Babylon); Christ enters the genealogy at the point of the exile, making him the one who comes out of the depths of Israel's judgment to bring restoration.

4.note: I am the Alpha and the Omega, who is and who was and who is to come, the Almighty — God's self-declaration in Isaiah 41:4 ('I, the LORD, the first and with the last; I am he') is directly applied to Christ in Revelation 1:8 and Isaiah 22:13; the divine name 'first and last' given to the God who raises up the nations in Isaiah is claimed by the risen Jesus.

I am the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last, the beginning and the end — Jesus explicitly claims the title from Isaiah 41:4 / Isaiah 44:6 / Isaiah 48:12; this is one of Revelation's clearest identifications of Jesus with the LORD of Isaiah.

8.note: Abraham believed God and it was counted to him as righteousness, and he was called a friend of God — James cites Genesis 15:6 and applies the title 'friend of God' from Isaiah 41:8 (Abraham, my friend); both texts ground Abraham's unique standing in faith.

You are my friends if you do what I command you — Jesus's elevation of the disciples to friends echoes the title God gives Abraham in Isaiah 41:8; what was unique to Abraham (called my friend) is now extended through Christ to all who abide in him.

10.note: Do not be afraid, for I am with you — the LORD's reassurance to Israel in Isaiah 41:10 is repeated to Paul in a night vision at Corinth (Acts 18:9-10: 'Do not be afraid... for I am with you'); the covenant presence-promise of Isa 41 is extended through Christ to Paul's missionary work among the Gentiles.

I am with you always, to the end of the age — Jesus's parting promise echoes the covenant reassurance formula of Isaiah 41:10 (I am with you); the presence that Israel received from the LORD is now given by the risen Christ to the church in its mission to all nations.

14.note: Who gave himself for us to redeem us from all lawlessness — the title Redeemer (Isaiah 41:14: your Redeemer is the Holy One of Israel) is fulfilled in Christ who gives himself as the ransom; the Holy One of Israel who redeems the worm Jacob is the same Holy One who takes human flesh to redeem his people from iniquity.

18.note: I will open rivers on the bare heights and fountains in the midst of the valleys; I will make the wilderness a pool of water — the water-in-the-desert promise of Isaiah 41:18 feeds into Jesus's Tabernacles declaration (John 7:38: rivers of living water from the believer); the Spirit is the water that transforms the dry land.

2.note: When you pass through the waters, I will be with you; when you walk through fire you shall not be burned — the promise of Isaiah 43:2 is typologically demonstrated by Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego in Daniel 3:25, where a figure 'like a son of God' accompanies them in the furnace; the presence of God in trial points forward to Christ's presence with his people in suffering.

Do not be surprised at the fiery trial when it comes upon you to test you — Peter's encouragement to persecuted Christians draws on the OT pattern of God's people passing through fire and water (Isaiah 43:2); the promise that God is present in the trial is the ground of endurance under suffering.

3.note: I am the LORD your God, the Holy One of Israel, your Savior; I give Egypt as your ransom — the ransom-language of Isaiah 43:3 anticipates 1 Timothy 2:6 (Christ Jesus, who gave himself as a ransom for all); the God who ransomed Israel from Egypt gives his Son as the ransom for the world.

10.note: Unless you believe that I am he you will die in your sins — Jesus's ego eimi declaration in John 8:24 directly echoes Isaiah 43:10 ('I am he') and Isaiah 43:13; the absolute 'I am' without predicate in John 8 is a claim to the divine name of Isa 43, identifying Jesus with the LORD who alone is God and Savior.

Before Abraham was, I am — Jesus's climactic ego eimi in John 8:58 draws on the timeless divine self-predication of Isaiah 43:10-13 (you are my witnesses... that I am he; before me no god was formed); the pre-existence claim echoes the LORD's assertion of eternal uniqueness in Isaiah.

18.note: Remember not the former things, nor consider the things of old; behold I am doing a new thing — Paul's declaration (if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; the old has passed away; behold, the new has come) is the direct fulfillment of Isaiah 43:18-19; the new thing God promised through the Servant is the new creation inaugurated in Christ's resurrection.

Behold, I am doing a new thing — Revelation 21:5 (Behold, I am making all things new) is the eschatological completion of Isaiah 43:19; what Isa 43 declared as an imminent new act of God is fulfilled in stages: first in the return from exile, then in Christ's resurrection, and finally in the new creation of Rev 21.

25.note: I, I am he who blots out your transgressions for my own sake — when Jesus declares 'your sins are forgiven' (Mark 2:5), the scribes ask 'Who can forgive sins but God alone?' (Mark 2:7); they correctly recall Isaiah 43:25, where only God says 'I blot out your transgressions'; Jesus's claim to forgive is a claim to the divine prerogative of Isa 43.

He who blots out your transgressions — the divine act of erasing sin in Isaiah 43:25 is accomplished in Christ through the cross; Colossians 2:14 describes God canceling the record of debt by nailing it to the cross, the definitive enactment of what Isaiah 43:25 promised: a God who blots out transgression for his own sake.

4.note: Even to your old age I am he, even to gray hairs I will carry you — the God who carries his people through every stage of life and never lets them fall is the Good Shepherd: no one can snatch them from my hand, and no one can snatch them from my Father's hand (John 10:28-29); the carrying God of Isaiah is the carrying Shepherd of John.

I have made you, and I will carry you; I will sustain and rescue you — the divine constancy of care across the whole lifespan anticipates Hebrews 13:5 (Never will I leave you; never will I forsake you) and Matthew 28:20 (I am with you always, to the end of the age); the one who carries from birth to old age is the one who promises perpetual presence.

9.note: I am God, and there is no other; I am God, and there is none like me — the monotheistic foundation Paul builds on in 1 Corinthians 8:6 (for us there is but one God, the Father, from whom all things came and for whom we live; and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom all things came) rests on this Isaianic declaration; the one God revealed in Christ is the God Isaiah proclaims here.

10.note: Declaring the end from the beginning, and from ancient times things not yet done, saying: my purpose will stand — the God who declares the end from the beginning is the one who calls himself the Alpha and Omega, the First and the Last, the Beginning and the End (Revelation 22:13); Christ assumes this title (Revelation 1:8,17; Isaiah 22:13), claiming the divine prerogative of commanding all history from its source to its goal.

My purpose will stand and I will accomplish all that I please — the divine purposefulness that controls history includes the cross: Jesus was handed over by God's deliberate plan and foreknowledge (Acts 2:23); what looked like the enemy's triumph was God's eternal purpose being executed precisely on schedule, the end declared from the beginning.

13.note: I am bringing my righteousness near — it is not far off — and my salvation will not linger — the imminent arrival of divine righteousness and salvation is the theme of Paul's gospel: the righteousness of God has been made known, to which the Law and the Prophets testify (Romans 3:21-22); what Isaiah sees approaching in the distance, Paul announces has arrived in the death and resurrection of Christ.

My salvation will not linger; I will grant salvation in Zion — the salvation that does not linger is the once-for-all offering of Christ: Christ was sacrificed once to take away the sins of many; and he will appear a second time, not to bear sin, but to bring salvation to those who are waiting for him (Hebrews 9:28); Isaiah's approaching salvation becomes Hebrews' two-advent framework.

12.note: Jesus says 'I am the first and the last' to John on Patmos, directly claiming YHWH's self-identification from Isaiah 48:12; the same formula recurs in Revelation 2:8 and Revelation 22:13 ('the first and the last, the beginning and the end'), anchoring Christ's eternal divine identity in Isaiah's monotheistic claim.

The risen Christ's final self-description — 'I am the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last, the beginning and the end' — is the fullest NT expansion of Isaiah 48:12's formula, identifying the returning Lord as the eternal God who called Israel from the beginning.

16.note: Isaiah 48:16b 'the Lord GOD has sent me, and his Spirit' is the Servant's claim to be Spirit-sent by the Father — the same Trinitarian structure Christ enacts when he reads from Isaiah 61:1-2 in the Nazareth synagogue ('The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me... he has sent me').

6.note: The Servant's 'I gave my back to those who strike, and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard; I hid not my face from disgrace and spitting' is fulfilled at Jesus's trial and passion — Matthew 26:67 (spit on and struck) and John 19:1 (flogged) enact this self-offering verse by verse.

The flogging of Jesus by Pilate's soldiers directly fulfills 'I gave my back to those who strike' (Isaiah 50:6); Peter cites the Servant Songs as a unified passion narrative in 1 Peter 2:22-25, confirming that the early church read Isa 50 as messianic prophecy.

8.note: Paul's 'Who shall bring any charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies' echoes the Servant's courtroom challenge in Isaiah 50:8 ('He who vindicates me is near. Who will contend with me?'); the forensic logic — vindication by God making every accusation unanswerable — is carried directly into Paul's no-condemnation declaration for those in Christ.

6.note: Isaiah 51:6 'the heavens will vanish like smoke, the earth will wear out like a garment' is part of the same tradition that Hebrews quotes from Psalms 102:26 when proving Christ's eternal immutability — the perishing creation is contrasted with the eternal Son who 'remains' and whose 'years will have no end' (Hebrews 1:11-12).

Peter's description of the day of the Lord — 'the heavens will pass away with a roar and the heavenly bodies will be burned up and dissolved' — draws on the same cosmic dissolution imagery as Isaiah 51:6, both contrasting the passing creation with the eternal salvation that Christ secures.

12.note: YHWH's 'I, I am he who comforts you' (Isaiah 51:12) prefigures Christ's promise of 'another Helper' (Parakletos) — the Spirit of comfort sent in Christ's name (John 14:16-17,26). The personal divine comfort that Isaiah declares becomes the Trinitarian comfort of the new covenant, mediated through Christ and actualized by the Spirit.

17.note: Isaiah 51:17 'You who have drunk from the hand of the LORD the cup of his wrath' and v.22 'I have taken from your hand the cup of staggering' together form the OT template for Christ's Gethsemane prayer ('let this cup pass from me') and his voluntary drinking of it — Christ drained the cup of divine judgment so that it could be removed from his people (Isaiah 51:22-23).

15.note: He has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate — Mary's Magnificat echoes Isaiah 57:15 (the high and lofty One who inhabits eternity also dwells with the contrite and lowly in spirit); the pattern of God exalting the lowly and dwelling with the humble reaches its climax in the incarnation.

The Word became flesh and tabernacled among us — the high and lofty One who dwells with the lowly and contrite (Isaiah 57:15) takes his dwelling-with-the-humble to its ultimate expression in the incarnation; the eternal God who inhabits eternity pitches his tent in human flesh.

19.note: He came and preached peace to you who were far off and peace to those who were near — Paul directly cites Isaiah 57:19 in Ephesians 2:17 as the scriptural basis for Christ's reconciling work; the promise of peace to both the far (Gentiles) and the near (Jews) is fulfilled in Christ who broke down the dividing wall between the two.

20.note: The wicked are like the tossing sea that cannot be quiet; its waters toss up mire and dirt — Isaiah 57:20's image of the restless, defiling sea anticipates Jude 13's description of false teachers as wild waves of the sea, casting up the foam of their own shame; the spiritual instability of wickedness is figured in both texts as uncontrollable water.

6.note: To loose the bonds of wickedness, to undo the straps of the yoke, to let the oppressed go free — Jesus's Nazareth manifesto (Luke 4:18) combines Isaiah 61:1 and Isaiah 58:6; the liberating ministry Jesus claims as his own draws from both texts; the fast God chooses (Isaiah 58:6) is social justice, and Jesus embodies it as the one who sets the captive free.

7.note: Share your bread with the hungry, bring the homeless poor into your house, cover the naked — the ethical demands of Isaiah 58:7 are the exact list Jesus uses to describe service to himself in Matthew 25:35-40 (I was hungry and you gave me food, I was a stranger and you welcomed me, I was naked and you clothed me); the commands of Isa 58 are fulfilled in service to Christ.

8.note: Then shall your light break forth like the dawn — the light breaking forth as the reward of righteous practice (Isaiah 58:8) anticipates Jesus's declaration 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12) and his command that his disciples let their light shine (Matthew 5:16); the dawn-light of Isa 58 is realized in the incarnation and reproduced in the life of those who follow the Light.

11.note: You shall be like a watered garden, like a spring of water whose waters do not fail — the spring-that-does-not-fail of Isaiah 58:11 feeds into Jesus's promise to the Samaritan woman (John 4:14: the water that I will give will become a spring of water welling up to eternal life); the covenant promise of unceasing water is fulfilled in the Spirit given through Christ.

Out of his heart will flow rivers of living water — the never-failing spring of Isaiah 58:11 (and the wells of salvation in Isaiah 12:3) lie behind Jesus's Tabernacles declaration; the Spirit as living water makes the believer a spring that never runs dry, the covenant blessing of Isa 58 extended through Christ.

13.note: If you turn back your foot from the Sabbath, from doing your pleasure on my holy day — the Sabbath-as-delight in Isaiah 58:13-14 resonates with Jesus's declaration that the Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath (Mark 2:27-28); Jesus does not abolish the Sabbath but fulfills its deepest intent as the one who gives true rest.

14.note: Then you shall take delight in the LORD, and I will make you ride on the heights of the earth — the Sabbath-rest of Isaiah 58:13-14 is the OT type of the eschatological rest that Hebrews 4:10-11 describes: whoever has entered God's rest has ceased from his works just as God did from his; the heights-riding delight of Isa 58 is fulfilled in the rest Christ gives to those who come to him (Matthew 11:28-30).

5.note: Isaiah 62:5 'as the bridegroom rejoices over the bride, so shall your God rejoice over you' is the OT anticipation of Revelation 19:7 ('the marriage of the Lamb has come, and his Bride has made herself ready') — Jesus is explicitly identified as the divine bridegroom who rejoices over his redeemed people, bringing the prophetic image to its consummation.

John the Baptist applies the bridegroom figure to Jesus: 'The friend of the bridegroom, who stands and hears him, rejoices greatly at the bridegroom's voice' — the rejoicing bridegroom of Isaiah 62:5 is the same one whose voice John hears and over whom he rejoices.

11.note: Christ's return announcement — 'Behold, I am coming soon, bringing my recompense with me, to repay each one for what he has done' — is the NT fulfillment of Isaiah 62:11's 'Behold, your salvation comes; behold, his reward is with him, and his recompense before him'; the verbal parallel is exact and intentional.

The triumphal entry quotation 'Say to the daughter of Zion, Behold, your king is coming to you' (Matthew 21:5) draws its opening formula from Isaiah 62:11 ('Say to the daughter of Zion, Behold, your salvation comes'), identifying the entering king as the salvation Isa 62 announces.

12.note: Peter's description of the church — 'a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession' — draws on Isaiah 62:12's titles for redeemed Israel ('The Holy People, The Redeemed of the LORD, A City Not Forsaken'), applying the prophetic identity of restored Zion to the NT community gathered around Christ.
